schedule_event.title: Opening welcome reception
schedule_event.description: All delegates are invited to join us for the opening welcome reception from 7.30pm on Tuesday 1 September in the Royal Geographical Society (with IBG) Marquee.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Arboreal-human intra-actions: A walkshop to explore living with trees in West London
schedule_event.description:
This walkshop* engages with questions of urban socio-environmental justice through an embodied exploration of living with London’s trees.

Whilst walking through the streets around the RGS-IBG conference venue in West London, participants will be invited to tune into their embodied and affective experiences of what it is like to live with trees.

By UN standards, London is a forest, with a canopy cover of 21% (Lösing, 2025). Recognising trees' role in mitigating the impacts of the climate emergency alongside their social and health benefits, the city has ambitions to increase that cover by 10% in the next decades (London Urban Forest Partnership, 2020). Yet trees are not evenly distributed, with global-majority communities and lower-income groups across the UK disproportionately more likely to live in neighbourhoods with a paucity of canopy cover (Friends of the Earth, 2023; The Woodland Trust, 2023).

In these contexts, this walkshop posits trees as lively entities, intra-acting with humans and other more-than-humans (Barad, 2007; Bennett, 2010), shaping their environment whilst also being shaped by it and by anthropogenic/anthropocenic actions. The walkshop asks participants to think about what tree equity feels like, using The Woodland Trust’s Tree Equity Score Map to compare this West London site to other places. It also prompts participants to consider the ways in which austerity measures in the UK have impacted on the planting and maintenance of trees (Iozzi, 2025; Rotherham and Flinders, 2019; Woudstra and Allen, 2022).

In addition, participants will have the opportunity to 'listen like a tree' using the more-than-human community toolkit, to further sensitise ourselves to the lives of trees.

Methodologically, the walkshop will invite participants to reflect on what mobile, embodied, affective and collective methods can offer our engagements with questions of socio-environmental (in)justice in urban spaces (Bates and Rhys-Taylor, 2017; Rondel and Henneke, 2024).

As an exploratory event, we invite participants to bring their own knowledges and expertises, to share and reflect.

*walking is understood as walking or wheeling or any kind of moving through space that can be done by any body at any speed (Rose, 2025).

Practicalities

The walkshop will take 2 ½ hours, including a 1-hour walk and a 30-minute outdoor activity. There will be a short briefing session ahead of the walk and time for reflections afterwards. We will invite you to document your experiences and reflections, and these can be uploaded (photos, sound-recordings, videos, or text) to a shared Padlet.

The route will be approximately 3km long with pauses to rest. We will be moving on pavements and paved paths, with a possible short section on paths in Hyde Park. We will be crossing roads at designated crossing points with dropped kerbs and/or pedestrian crossings. Some of the roads will be busy with motorised traffic and cyclists. The footways will be busy with pedestrians. Depending on the weather, pollution levels may be high.

Places are limited, please sign up in advance

When signing up, please let us know of any accessibility requirements and we can explore supporting your participation.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of slowing down, rehumanising and challenging inequalities in the leisure and tourism academy
Women in Economic Geography Social Hour
schedule_event.description:
The Women in Economic Geography Social Hour will be held on Tuesday, 1 Sept, 6-8pm at The Queen's Arms (30 Queen's Gate Mews, South Kensington, London SW7 5QL).

All women and non-binary folks who work in or around the sub-discipline are encouraged to attend, meet, and network over drinks and snacks in a warm and welcoming space.

Organised by Alkim Karaagac, Felicia Liu, Jessa Loomis, Beki McElvain

Sponsored by the Gender and Feminist Geographies Research Group (GFGRG), the Economic Geography Research Group (EGRG), and the journal Economic Geography.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Chair's opening keynote: The shape of inequality and the weight of the world
schedule_event.description:
At a time of political upheaval, in an age of mistrust, on the cusp of unprecedented technological change, it is easy to lose sight of the shape of inequality, and the cost of going with the flow.

This presentation reflects on what has gone wrong, whether there is hope, and how to turn it into a plan.

paper.title: The Shape of Inequality and the Weight of the World

schedule_event.title: Newcomer's event
schedule_event.description: Join us for an introduction to the conference and to the conference venue. All are welcome: those who are new to the conference are particularly encouraged to attend.

schedule_event.title: Making geography matter in turbulent times
schedule_event.description:
In a period marked by significant uncertainty and rapid change—whether arising from shifting geopolitical relations, the escalating climate emergency, the cost‑of‑living crisis, or the increasingly precarious position of higher education—we find ourselves working and teaching within profoundly turbulent times. Geography, as a discipline centrally concerned with spatial relationships, power, environment, and society, is uniquely positioned to engage with these challenges. While there is broad consensus that geography matters, an enduring question remains: how do we effectively demonstrate its relevance and value to young people, policymakers, communities, and our own institutions?

This seminar, hosted by GeogEd, provides a timely opportunity to reflect on and critically discuss the ongoing and future relevance of geography in addressing contemporary global and local challenges. The event will also mark the 50th anniversary of the Journal of Geography in Higher Education (JGHE), celebrating its longstanding contribution to advancing scholarship, pedagogy, and debate in geography education, and its role in shaping this agenda over the past five decades.

Bringing together colleagues from across the diverse spectrum of geographical research, teaching, and practice, the seminar will create space to explore how we are collectively working to make geography matter—intellectually, socially, and institutionally—both now and in the future. The programme will combine a series of short presentations with a participatory workshop, encouraging dialogue, exchange of ideas, and collaborative reflection.

Participants are invited to submit an abstract for a 10‑minute Lightning Talk to contribute to the afternoon seminar on the theme Making geography matter in turbulent times. These Lightning Talks will form the basis for subsequent discussion and will be followed by a workshop facilitated by Professor David Higgitt, Editor‑in‑Chief of JGHE.

Colleagues who wish to attend and/or contribute to the seminar (online or in person)are warmly encouraged to complete the Making Geography Matter in Turbulent Times: GeogEd Pre-RGS Seminar Registration – Fill in form by 27 June 2026

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Politicising Political Geography: Bridges, Dialogues, Solidarity
schedule_event.description: This event is a space for academics from across the field of political geography to take part in important conversations about the world and the discipline. PolGRG members are eager to build on ongoing conversations within the geographical community and explore how we translate these into real-world outcomes. Themes will cover academic responsibility, the place of research and higher education in a changing geopolitical conjuncture, the newly emboldened Islamophobias, racisms, and far-right politics in and beyond the UK, and other urgent issues. Rather than an extension of the conference’s predominant panel layout, this workshop will incorporate various formats, such as participatory exercises, roundtables, and article discussions. There would also be space for conversations around professional development, navigating the discipline, and teaching political geography.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Modelling Bodies/Modelling Cities
schedule_event.description: An interactive workshop with parallel sessions including the opportunity to explore the Royal Geographical Society and Kensington area by using a smartphone-enabled 3D modelling app, view an exhibition of a physical city model layered with real-time data, as well as contribute to a lively discussion on urban digital visualities (TBC).
